title: 2AM at Paradise Cafe
notes:
Oh Barry, why couldn't you do more jazz???? How dare you give us the best cafe jazz album ever only to never return to the genre?? Oh right, this is a review. This album has everything you could want in a jazz record. Mostly comprising of melancholic blues, it varies quite a bit on vibe of each song, which I think makes it quite replayable. Free-flowing (my biggest achilies heel) transitions between songs make this album sound like it was a single take, further highlighting the musicanship of the songs. Each song is a small vignet of someone you might find at a jazz bar at 2AM. They tend to deal with tragic love because who else is in a jazz bar at 2AM?

Low points in the album for me are really only the variety. I think the album achieves everything it sets out to do, exceptionally well. But it does sometimes sound like 11 blues songs in a row. Maybe slotting in some different styles would break this album up and make it much more replayable for many. For my taste, there is a lot of replayability because of two things. The first are the big, high energy moments of some ballads like *Say No More*. The second is the choice of keys and vibe switch to songs like *Blue*, which are for all purposes slow blues, it feels different than other tracks because it wanders in and out of major keys and plays the swing differently, sitting deep in the pocket while playing straight eighth piano riffs.

Highlights are the two features (*Big City Blues featuring Mel Torme* and *Blue featuring Sarah Vaughan*) as well as *Say No More*. *Say No More* draws an incredible energy chart, waiting for its big moment and delivering stunning, emotional vocals. The features have distinct sounds, but the best part of both of the tracks for me is the harmony. Both trade verses 1 and 2 and harmonize the 3rd verse, which really juxtaposes the characters of the song. Sarah Vaughan and Barry Manilow harmonizing, almost as if they are saying the words to each other, is a great example.
